risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomisation was by computer allocation using the web-based randomisation service of the Norwich Clinical Trials Unit. ... a stratified randomisation with 3 strata ... In each stratum, balanced blocks of random length (4, 6 or 8) were fitted to assign an equal number of SPC + MP and SPC participants." Comment: This describes a clearly random allocation process using a computer-based web randomisation service, stratification, and random block lengths, which is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as truly random.

Quote: "Randomisation was by computer allocation using the web-based randomisation service of the Norwich Clinical Trials Unit." Comment: A web-based randomisation service is an explicit central allocation mechanism that prevents the recruiter from knowing the next assignment before enrollment, so allocation concealment is adequately described.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Due to the nature of the intervention, blinding of participants, physiotherapists and researchers was not feasible." Comment: Participants and personnel were explicitly not blinded, and the study outcomes included self-reported symptom severity and quality of life measures that could plausibly be influenced by awareness of allocation, so performance bias is high across the study overall.

Quote: "Due to the nature of the intervention, blinding of participants, physiotherapists and researchers was not feasible." and "several standardised self-report measures were used to assess participants’ symptoms of incontinence ... quality of life ... and self-esteem". Comment: The outcomes were primarily patient self-report questionnaires, so the patient was effectively the outcome assessor. Because participants were not blinded, knowledge of allocation could influence how they reported subjective symptoms, quality of life, and self-esteem. Therefore detection bias is high risk.

Quote: "From the 36 women allocated to the intervention (SPC + MP) group and the 37 women allocated to the control (SPC group), 14 and 10 women respectively were removed from the complete case analysis for the following reasons: failed to attend the scheduled treatments (10 SPC + MP; 7 SPC), medical reasons (1 SPC + MP; 1 SPC), lost to follow-up (3 SPC + MP; 2 SPC)." Comment: Attrition was moderate but not excessive overall (24/73, about 33%), and it was broadly similar between groups with reasons that were mostly logistical or follow-up-related rather than clearly outcome-related. The paper also notes that the intervention-group withdrawals were “most likely to be due to aspects of delivery, for example the timing of the classes and geographical location, rather than the nature of the exercises,” which reduces concern that missingness biased the comparison.
